speaker: interviewer
text: The farmer brought you some liquor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't remember much about that. I think-- that was it happened that I got pregnant. I think so because it was our honeymoon, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: We run away from concentration camp. And that was the first time that we were together. So it was. And we were very careful. But I didn't know I was pregnant. I didn't know at all. And I remember that my stomach went up and down when [INAUDIBLE]. And he say, you imagined. My stomach goes up and down, too. So I had no-- And I was very childish. I didn't know-- was the youngest [INAUDIBLE] from the family. And I really didn't know. And so the first day when I got freed, and I, I couldn't walk. Now it was already [INAUDIBLE] when I get to the park, when we were hidden by the pigs. I couldn't walk. And my legs were sore. And I couldn't walk. And I didn't understand it because I was very sporty. And I, you know-- I was never sick and could do everything. So-- and then, when the-- it was this way. We were hidden, and we had a little hole. And we could look very far. And we could hear the front for the German. You could hear the shooting. We were by the [INAUDIBLE], just by the-- in Ukraine, is where-- Ukraine's border. And you could hear shooting. And you could hear it already coming to you. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's now spring of 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, spring, 1944. And also-- so we, we-- and everybody came to the farmer. His family came to-- also the, the farm where we were-- first farm where we were. And he came to them. And I remember it was a little boy. Oh, yeah. We were laying there still. And nobody was allowed to know it. Well, we didn't know what would happen. And, and little boy ran after a bird. And he ran. And the bird went on top of us. And he ran on top of us. And he saw us. And he ran outside and he tells everybody what is there. But thank god. And then also, we were afraid when the-- When she found out that I was pregnant, she want to send me away. And, and I didn't know where did-- [INAUDIBLE] where to go. And-- [INAUDIBLE] we didn't know what to do. I want to have an abortion, but who will give you an-- we, we, we didn't know. And I said to her, I am not pregnant. I, I know-- I didn't know. Because I didn't have my menstruation from the moment that I went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Camp. I never had it. So I said to her, it's impossible. And I didn't believe that I was pregnant. So that helped. And also, her husband liked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so that helped. So we didn't send away. But then this little boy came on top of us. So thank god the-- we saw-- the German-- we hear the front coming nearer. And we saw the Germans running away. Far away, you could see the Germans running away. And then one day, Chaim went down and the Russian came already. I went down and saying, oh, he's running away from me. [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE]. Oh, he went down. So he went down. And then, he came back up. And we could go down. And then we saw the Russians and we-- and we had-- kissed the Russians. That's what I remember. And then also, the family went back to their places. And then I couldn't walk. And I got that big. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were free. We are-- we're not really free because, first of all, I was pregnant. Six months. We went to a doctor in town, a [INAUDIBLE] man that was student. And we had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Poland by the Russian-- by the Ukrainian border. I didn't speak the language. We had no money. Well, the farmer would give us, of course, nothing back. And we-- Chaim helped him a few days there. They got a little bit of work, learned to work a little bit. We both didn't work nine months long. And we didn't get much to eat. And so the farmer heard that, that they were against-- that he took the Jews. And he say, you know, I hear something that they want to kill you anyway. So let me bring you to Chelm. We were not far from Chelm. Chelm is a very famous town in the Jewish history. Sholem Aleichem wrote a lot about Chelm. The Chelm [NON-ENGLISH]. So we went to Chelm. And Chaim and I, we thought we were the only Jews left. Because we thought every concentration camp was like Sobibor. But Sobibor was a death camp. And everybody that came went straight to the gas chamber. Just a few people what worked there. Just 125 people that worked there. Only when the Germans worked-- came over to the, the, the army, when were-- to the Russian-- to the Polish border, the, the ghetto, the Jews from ghetto from Minsk came to Sobibor. And somehow, on the end-- a month before we had the uprising, we had about 600 of a month or six week. I don't know any time so much. We had 600 people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the uprising was there, there were 600 Jews there. So that's the reason the operation was so big, and everybody was running away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the most time we were in camp, were only 125 Jews there. So Chaim and I, we thought that everybody-- that everything would, would have been-- every camp was like Sobibor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came to Chelm, a lot of Jews came already to Chelm. And it was a house from a Jewish family that were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came there together. Everybody came there together. Not everybody. I don't-- and we slept together in, in a room so big-- a very small room. And we did-- we had-- because I was pregnant, they gave us a bed to sleep on. And we didn't own anything. We didn't have anything. We didn't-- I cried so much. [INAUDIBLE] That is freedom. We didn't have to eat and just buy some juice. We had money already. And I did cooking. I started cooking for all the people were there to give myself food. I remember I have a diary. And I write in it that I burnt my leg. And I could almost not walk. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is May or June of 1945 about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. Yeah, it was in that time. I don't know. That was something in that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When the-- so that was in that-- yeah. And oh, it was horrible. And, and I remember also, they went to Sobibor back. All the people went to Sobibor and went in the ground and found money, lots of money. Because that's what all the Jews did-- the, the, the Polish Jews most. Not the Dutch Jews. They didn't think about it. And they'd hide money in the ground. So they were killing each other there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, Chaim, you don't go back. We made it so far. You know? So we didn't know what to do. And then Chaim had to go in the army. The Polish want to take Chaim in the army. So Chaim started working in the hospital as nurse. And he is the type, he's so weak, he couldn't even see blood. So I remember there was a train accident, and he had to help all these people. He said to me, Selma, I cannot do it. And also, it didn't help. So it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then also once-- there's a very interesting story. That gold what-- and the money that they found in Sobibor, they brought to Chelm. And once they told-- somebody told Chaim that he had some $100 bills. And the number is gone, if Chaim knows somebody where he can change it. So he gave Chaim a $100 bill. And Chaim said, I know [INAUDIBLE]. I know somebody that can perhaps change it and can do something about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we put it under our hat. And in the morning, we woke up. The $100 was gone. So if-- what Chaim-- the money that we made, a little bit what Chaim made-- I don't know about gasoline. I don't remember what he did really. And I started sewing by hand for people. He give to the man for the $100. We had to pay that off. 15 years later, we are in Israel. And-- 10 years later. And we have all the gathering from the Jews from Sobibor. And I see the man with the $100 bill. And I say to him, do you remember that you stole the $100 under our hat? He said, yes, I remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, he was in Israel by our house. I say, you owe me $100.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. What a story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the end for now. We're gathering together with people from Sobibor. Yeah. Can you imagine?

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway-- So Chaim is in the army. He's working in the army. You're very--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Chaim is not in the army. He is working there by the-- by a hospital. So he went out of that. And we started going away from Chelm, because we thought that was no-- that was not-- that is no solution. So we started going away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we met somebody. [INAUDIBLE] was his name. And we met somebody somehow, and he was from Sobibor. And he said, you know what you have to do? Go to [INAUDIBLE], a little town. And grow a mustache and change your name. And that's what we did. What he did. We went to that [INAUDIBLE]. We traveled by trucks, on trucks from one place to the other. And I was like that. And it was terrible, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had no roof above our head. I had no clothes. I had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walk-- and we went to that little village. And there was a Jewish woman, and she owned some old, little houses. Miserable houses. But she say, you could stay in one of these rooms. So we went to [INAUDIBLE]. And we stayed in a little room. And Chaim made a bed for--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were old houses. And he took wood from the old houses and he built it in beds. And he built a little bed for-- and he finished the rooms a little bit. There was-- when there was big holes in it, it was in between. It started to get cold already for-- winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: We stayed there a half year. We stayed-- And you changed your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: We changed our name to Chaim's mother's, [INAUDIBLE]. And then he went away. We need to live. We had no money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Chaim went to other-- other towns where the Germans moved away. And he bought shoe laces. I don't know what he bought. I really was so involved in my own misery that-- he bought things, and then came back and sold it in our area. And-- and so-- so he was away a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and, and one-- he was not away, then I felt that I had-- I got the baby. When I felt that I had-- I didn't know how-- if you have pain in the back, [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't know anything how to get a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I got pain in my stomach, I think. And we had to walk five miles. And we came to a school. And there was-- there was-- all Germans were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no German-- were Polish doctors were with the Germans. They were very antisemitic in Poland. Anyway, I was five days and five nights in labor. And they didn't look at me. And they didn't want to know anything about me, and if-- because I had [INAUDIBLE]. I still had the [INAUDIBLE]. They wanted-- The farmer didn't buy enough ointment so that it-- there is an ointment when you put it on enough that it-- that you kill these germs. So, so they didn't want to know anything about me. And I was laying there. And I didn't know what to do. There were Polish women, but I couldn't speak with them. They told me that I had to push and push and push. And they told me-- but they speak Polish. And I didn't speak a word Polish. So finally, the head was showing. And one doctor came. And oh, yeah. In between, he said to me, well, you don't stop screaming. Well, I was in tremendous pain. Then I throw you out on the streets he told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I had to walk to a room. And I got the baby. And I brought-- walked back to the room with the baby. And it was a normal baby. It was a very cute, little baby. And I had lice, too, still I think. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was there in that hospital. And they didn't take care of me. And they didn't do anything to me. And finally, Chaim got a note that the baby was there. And he came with a wagon and he picked me up. And we went to that little apartment. And finally, we-- I, I got more ointment. And-- And also, then [INAUDIBLE] the girl that I met in jail in Amsterdam--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend. And we were buddies in Sobibor. We slept next to each other. So she was-- she was also alive. And she came to live with us. So that was already a little bit easier when I was not always alone. When I-- when Chaim went away, I cried so much that the neighbors-- the walls were very thin. They made the same noise that I was crying. I was so lonely. I couldn't speak with anybody. And of course, with few Polish Jews, I couldn't speak fit. They spoke Yiddish. And I didn't know Yiddish. So it was-- it was horrible. I-- It was not so bad as concentration camp. But I still was very-- Chaim wasn't there. So I was all alone. Finally, [? Ula ?] comes to live with us. And we didn't have anything. So Chaim made also a bed for her. And we had to get a warm pair of boots. And we had to get her the underwear. And we would get her a warm coat. And when she went outside, I couldn't go outside. So somehow, we were happy because we were-- had each other already. And we had this baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one night we had to eat. And also, the Russian captains came to us. And they were nice Jews-- captain. Most of the Jews, a lot of Jews. And they were very nice to us. They came to us. And life got a little bit normal. But we were so poor. We had nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim built a bed for the-- [? Ula. ?] And the baby was still sleeping between us. And I had no blankets. And we had no blankets our self in the beginning. But we used our clothes as our blankets. So finally, a Jewish family [INAUDIBLE] feather bed they got back. And in the feather bed was fleas. You had no idea what it is. We put it on night, and the fleas came out. And I put it in the daytime out in the-- on-- and it freezing there. It's cold. But the fleas goes inside. Oh, was so horrible. So anyway-- We-- and you know, when we did the laundry together, we, we, we had a bowl and there was a haul in and another bowl to catch up the water. We had pans with holes in them. We closed it. Made bread. And then we would cook on the stove. We had no money to buy anything. We had no bathroom. There was no bathroom. We had a, a big pot in there. Everywhere-- and that was our bathroom. It was-- Oh, I remember once I was in an old house. I went to the bathroom. And the German was staying in back of me, a Polish soldier was staying in back of me. It was just-- it was so primitive in these villages. It was middle-- and we had no water. And [INAUDIBLE] in the little town w water. And in the winter, was snow and ice. And you had to go very far to get a little bit of water.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I come from Holland. Holland is very modern and-- Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we had warm water. We had showers. We had baths. And, and I had a-- I didn't know how to dress a baby. And I had to sew-- I had to sew clothes for the baby. I had to sew it myself from my own underwear, what I-- little bit what we had. And the windows were such holes in that in the morning, when we woke up, everything was ice. And, and so we had to take-- had-- we got a little-- made a little bed for the baby from the bed-- for the baby that she had warmer. Ugh, it was a-- it was a miserable time. And we had a-- an-- and where we heating with was from the dust from wood. And, and when they put it on, then it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sawdust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sawdust. And it went open and everything was full of it. Ugh. It-- but still, we were alive. We were alive. And one day in January--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what year is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is in 1944. In January-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, but it was almost [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the uprising was '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was January '44. They told us that there were-- the few Jews left in [INAUDIBLE] they want to-- they want to kill. So middle of the night, we put everything in a-- together with all the Jews and we went to Lublin. Also, it was very interesting. Once, there was a fire. Chaim told me once-- and the houses are from wood. Here, in America, it's wood, too. In Holland, it was of stone. He told me when one house burned, then the whole village fell down. So there was a fire in [INAUDIBLE]. And I was panicking because we had the baby, and we had a bed, we had a stove.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [? Ula ?] and I, we put everything outside in the cold so the fire-- how they-- and when they-- there's no fire people, firemen there. They have little boxes, little cans. And if-- make the fire out like that. It was so primitive. It was after the war. And they suffered tremendous in the war, too. So-- So when we went away to Lublin, we didn't have anything. So we went to Lublin to a family. Also, a man that was from Sobibor. He had run away from Sobibor. He was one of the people who run away from Sobibor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, people run away from Sobibor. They worked in the woods. And they killed some Germans. Some Ukrainians had to take care of them. And they got away. But that time, they-- 10 people got killed because they run away. And we had to stay. The whole camp had to come together, and we had to see-- and we stay on a line. And we had to see how they killed these 10 people. And what will happen to us, what-- And that-- This, this man made another friend along the way. And you know, they died very young in the freedom. So we went to Lublin, and we went to his house. And he made already a lot of money. I don't know, he worked with the Russians. He bought [INAUDIBLE] sugar and sold it. So we stayed in his house. In his-- yeah, in his room. And we stayed in a very big house. More Jews were in that house. It was already freedom. We were free. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was already-- I'm a little bit confused from all the talking. That was already-- when did he go-- yeah, that was already '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now, we are already in 1945. And so we stayed in a big house. And one day, the-- Chaim was not there. And I had the baby in a little carriage. And a knock on the door. And the man's name was Portlevnik. And he say, Portlevnik. Open the door. Open the door. And they put the-- the lights were out. And everybody came with their money what they had and put it under the baby's mattress when they thought they will not do anything to the baby. But somehow, the people went away. We didn't open the door. And they killed all the rest of the Jews in the building what was there when they opened the doors, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a pogrom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was just some other [INAUDIBLE] of-- you know-- they-- there was no pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was Poles? Just the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just the Poles. Because-- but not everybody was wild. When we were in Chelm, all the Jews were sitting together. And everybody tells their story. So we told our story. And we were, of course, very bitter, because we didn't get to eat, and we didn't get to wash ourselves. And we had a very-- didn't-- Later years, we found out that these people where we were hidden were angels because it was something very dangerous what they did. But at the moment, we didn't. So we told the people the fact that we had money and where people had hidden it. And these people from the farmers, wanted-- they went there at night and stole all the money away from the farmers, what we give them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't know that. We heard it years and years later. So this farmer said, and all the people in the village say, see. You helped the Jews and look what they did. And-- so [INAUDIBLE] we really made it up to them. And we sent a, a, a lot of money there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a lot. We sent them $100 in Christmas and Easter and during the year. Everything what we can, we do it. So that's the reason I tell you that people were, were animals because they were in the partisans. And in the partisans, you have to go out and kill somebody to get a piece of meat or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're back in Lublin. You're staying with someone from Sobibor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, Chaim meets a Dutch man. And he tells him that going is a train with Dutch people. And they're going to-- over Romania, somewhere in the-- and going to Russia. And then, try to get over Russia, over the Black Sea, so back to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel with that news?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I didn't feel so good because I have-- again, what do you know? What do you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to the railroad station. And we went in a freight train. Met-- and they made two layers, on the bottom people and on the top people. And I don't know how many people. A lot of people. And we went with the train to Romania. We were in Chernivtsi. And then we stopped. And we-- and the train went, and it stopped. And train went. And we didn't-- no bathroom. We didn't get to-- and we had a little baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had the baby with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had, of course, the baby with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name was Emile?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we traveled with the baby. And when the train stopped, we quickly run out. Chaim went to the front to get some water that we can wash the diapers. Or I went in the front and he went to a farmer to get some milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK? And-- and so it-- so-- And on the trains, I remember, once he was busy with washing the dishes and the train started going. So we run down. We had a few dishes, what we had. And we went upstairs. And when-- met the-- met the-- met the-- in the train again. And it was miserable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we were in Chernivtsi. It was a very nice town. And we were in a place, and then the baby got sick. I had dysentery and the baby got sick, too. So I went to a Russian hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- there was nothing to eat there and there was nothing to-- and there was no medicine. And I stayed there and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I get [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The middle of the night, Chaim came. And said, Selma, the train is going farther. We have to go out. And so he had to sign us out. And we went to a farm at the train. And it took us 2 weeks or 10 days. And we came out in Russia, in Odessa. When we were in Odessa, that was in an-- under a Black Sea. And it was on vacation [INAUDIBLE] from the Russians. And it was very nice. There, we got the Red Cross. That's the first time that the Red Cross helped us. And the Red Cross--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us food. And they give us clothes. I didn't get too much because there were a lot of German-- not Jews-- no Jews, but they get much more than-- Anyway, we were already-- we got to eat. And we were-- and the, and the baby got better and was healthy. And I felt much better because we were there 3 months. And we got nourished better. It was a beautiful place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was there too? There was the father of Anne Frank was there. I didn't know there was-- that the father of Anne Frank would be so famous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Otto Frank was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Otto Frank was there. Was also one of the people there in that camp. But he was much older than I am, so I didn't talk much with him. And I didn't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How old are you now, Selma, with this young--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm now 73.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now with-- I mean, and the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: In camp? With the baby--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went in at 20--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were there three months in that camp. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then, one day-- it was a Dutch captain. And they say that Chaim was Dutch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a-- and Chaim was not allowed to speak. But he didn't speak Dutch, of course. A little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So Chaim was not allowed to speak. And they say that Chaim was Dutch, so he was smuggled out of Russia onto the boat. And I was very nervous. And I nourished-- nursed the baby myself. So-- so I-- I didn't have any food for the baby. So when we came in the-- in the-- shall I say that?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, anyway, we were not on the boat. Anyway, the baby was smuggled up on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, why were they after Chaim? Because he was Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was Polish. And Polish couldn't go out of the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. So he didn't have papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Had no-- no. We didn't have papers. We had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Chaim had to go. So Chaim was smuggled on the boat. And I didn't have any milk. But he was so nervous that he could come on the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the kitchen and I got some milk. And first, I think too heavy milk. I don't know what it was, but I give the baby milk to drink. And the baby got sick and died in 24 hours. So we thought we are in freedom on the way to Holland. And we were on the boat. And the baby got buried at the island Naxos near Greece. And was buried on the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eight days later, we were in Holland. We came in-- into Marseilles. And people were very nice to us. They made big dinners and was very nice to us. And then we went with the train to Belgian. And Belgian were very friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we came to Holland. And in Holland, say, what are you doing here? We have so many people already here. We have nothing to eat for ourselves. What are you doing here? Holland suffered tremendous in the war. Very different reaction.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this is the first help you've really gotten since, since being hidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: With the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: First time you're being helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where did you go back to in Holland? Did you find your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We, we went by the freight-- they brought us by the truck to my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your brother there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I whistle the family's whistle. And he looked-- and he was sick. And he looks out of the window, and he thought, I'm not only sick. I think I'm crazy, too, when he saw me walking with Chaim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were the only two survivors of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had only two survivors from the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what camp was he in, Selma?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not in camp. He was hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was hidden. That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was hidden. All the years, he was hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right. So he saw this vision and he really thought he was--

speaker: interviewee

speaker: interviewer
text: Had he heard where you'd been? Did he have any idea that you were in Sobibor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. No. No, nobody would.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? No. See, Sobibor was not written on Sobibor. They would say they went the Eastern part of Poland or something like that. So when we came to Holland, nobody believed that we were-- nobody had ever heard about Sobibor. So they didn't believe us. It took us a year talking to people and telling that it was Sobibor. And the [INAUDIBLE] and professor, Professor [INAUDIBLE]. And he wrote-- a historian.
